segments.title: Warrior as a two-decker ironclad counterfactual
segments.summary: The episode asks how HMS Warrior as a two-decker ironclad would change the Warrior moment, British industrial signaling, and later battleship development.
segments.body: The discussion captures the lecture's central counterfactual. Dr. Clarke frames the historical Warrior moment as both technological and industrial theater, then explores how choosing a two-decker ironclad could have pushed the Royal Navy toward separate ironclad battle and frigate fleets, altered public measures of naval power, and made later attempts to recreate a Warrior moment more overwhelming and expensive.

evidence.note: The opening states the Patreon counterfactual: HMS Warrior as a two-decker ironclad rather than a frigate, against a background of Royal Navy two-decker construction.
The transcript defines a Warrior moment as technological superiority backed by industrial production, not just one exceptional ship.
The counterfactual section argues a two-decker design could combine the new capabilities, create an ironclad battle fleet alongside the frigate fleet, and change naval development incentives.
The closing links the altered Warrior moment to later Dreadnought-style attempts to overwhelm rather than merely overmatch rival warships.
